text: The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical port for the laparoscopic camera, moving it frequently from left to right and right to left during the total proctocolectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses Babcock forceps to demonstrate the location of the ureter in the right lower quadrant. A 5mm harmonic scalpel is employed to perform the line of TOLT mobilization. The patient's head is oriented towards the right side of the screen, while the feet are towards the left, with the right ureter reflected down.

The surgeon uses two ports on the left side for right-hand mobilization, creating a mirror image operation with ports in the left upper and lower quadrants for right side dissection, and ports in the right upper and lower quadrants for left side dissection. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect around the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon uses two contralateral ports to facilitate dissection, occasionally adding an extra instrument for traction. The right ureter is exposed and cleared of surrounding tissue. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure, with vessel division for the total proctocolectomy occurring at varying times.

The surgeon uses the ENSEAL instrument to dissect the gastrocolic omentum, applying energy to separate the tissue. Subsequently, the transverse mesocolon is taken with an endoscopic linear cutter equipped with a vascular load stapler.

The spleen is visible in the left upper quadrant of the screen as the surgeon approaches the splenic flexure. The right colon has been mobilized, and the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for precise dissection along the left side of the colon. The left ureter is identified as the dissection progresses from the rectosigmoid junction towards the splenic flexure, with the line of Toldt being mobilized intracorporeally using laparoscopic instruments.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic dissection along the left side of the colon, with the small bowel visible under the mesentery as vessels are divided using energy sources. The procedure avoids incisions other than at the perineum and stoma site, focusing on staying close to the colon wall rather than approaching more proximal vessels.

The surgeon uses a vascular load and the ENSEAL instrument to connect the left and right colon up towards the splenic flexure. The left colon is visible under the left colonic mesentery and the marginal arcade. The middle colic artery is identified as the last structure to be taken, following the use of three vascular loads for the inferior mesenteric, iliocolic, and middle colic arteries. The entire abdominal colon is now mobilized.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and prepares to take the vessels along the terminal ileum to fashion the ileostomy. The approach to vessel division depends on the extraction method, either through the stoma site or intracorporeally.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis using an endo-loop after the stapler and clip failed. The dissection proceeds into the pelvis and pre-sacral space, entering the avascular plane between the sacrum and mesorectum. The areolar tissue is visible posteriorly, with the anus oriented towards the top midline of the screen and the patient's left side towards the left.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the left side, working in a circular motion around the presacral space while avoiding the mesorectum. Traction and counter-traction are applied to expose the areolar plane. A small nerve twig is visible on the left-hand side of the screen, which the surgeon reflects laterally.

The surgeon navigates around a large uterus, using a clamp to hold it up and reflect it out of the way, while working in the anterior plane. A nerve is visible on the left side, preserved despite the challenging anatomy.

The surgeon attempts to reflect the uterus towards the left-hand side to maintain clear visualization of the surgical field. The camera is repositioned through the lower quadrant port to assist in holding the uterus out of the way. The surgical team works anteriorly, carefully avoiding the uterus, and proceeds towards the right side, preparing to transition to the perineal phase of the procedure.

The surgeon transects the rectum and prepares to remove the specimen through the perineum. A blue load is used to create an ileostomy, ensuring the vascular pedicle on the iliocolic arcade is hemostatic. The ileum is positioned to reach the ileostomy site without tension. The procedure transitions to the perineal side for an intersphincteric proctectomy, with the specimen visibly extracted and the stoma pulled through the port site for maturation of the Brooke ileostomy.

The surgeon navigates the intersphincteric plane, with the uterus no longer obstructing the view. The entire colon, including the omentum and terminal ileum, is extracted through this plane. The right colon is visible as it emerges, and the remaining specimen is removed. The procedure resembles natural orifice surgery, with the final steps involving the extraction and maturation of the ileostomy.
